title: edaphos: Disruptive Algorithms for Digital Soil Mapping
description:
edaphos is a research-oriented R package implementing frontier algorithms for Digital Soil Mapping (DSM) beyond the regression-tree state of the art. Contributions are organised across six research pillars:

  1. Causal AI — DAG-backed backdoor adjustment + LLM knowledge-graph extraction (Gemma 4 via Ollama), demonstrated on 1095 real WoSIS Cerrado profiles (v1.4.0).
  2. Physics-Informed ML — parametric pedogenetic ODE + differentiable Neural ODE + covariate-conditioned hierarchical Neural ODE.
  3. 4D Pedometry — multi-layer stacked ConvLSTM with multi-step rollout, optional mass-balance physics loss, and a stochastic Ensemble Kalman Filter with optional Gaspari–Cohn localization for sequential assimilation of new in-situ observations, demonstrated on a real MOD13Q1 NDVI + NASA POWER precipitation and temperature 4D cube over a 2° Cerrado AoI (v1.5.0).
  4. Foundation Models — SimCLR / MoCo-v2 contrastive pre-training on unlabelled raster patches with a Zenodo-published Cerrado encoder.
  5. Autonomous Active Learning — hybrid cLHS + QRF policy with Physics-Informed rejection gates.
  6. Quantum ML — pure-R ZZFeatureMap simulator, quantum kernel Gram matrix, closed-form Quantum Kernel Ridge Regression, and an optional dispatch to IBM Quantum Runtime via qiskit.

v1.6.0 introduced a unified uncertainty API built around a common edaphos_posterior S3 class, a single uncertainty_calibrate() diagnostic (CRPS, PICP, MPIW, reliability curve) and per-pillar adapters.

v1.7.0 introduces a capstone cross-pillar vignette — “Uma decisão de amostragem sob incerteza no Cerrado” — that integrates all six pillars in a single field-campaign narrative, causally grounded in the generative DSM framework of Zhang & Wadoux (2026, Eur. J. Soil Sci., 77:e70284). The vignette demonstrates causal identification (P1), physics-informed depth modelling (P2), 4D assimilation (P3), landscape representation (P4), active-learning query (P5) and quantum-kernel uncertainty (P6) cooperating to recommend eight sampling locations with full uncertainty quantification.

v1.7.1 fixes a honesty gap in v1.7.0: each pillar is now calibrated against its native query type (scalar effect for P1, depth profile for P2, future map for P3, spatial CV map for P4, held-out map for P5, held-out regression for P6) instead of being forced into a single shared query that produced misleading domain-mismatched PICP numbers. The capstone vignette now shows both the naïve and the native tables side by side with a pedagogical explanation of why the latter is scientifically correct.

v1.7.2 ships a causal-discovery trio benchmark comparing three strategies (expert / LLM-augmented / data-driven bnlearn hc-tabu-pc-stable) on 1 095 WoSIS Cerrado profiles, showing how the backdoor adjustment set varies from 0 to 6 covariates across methods — an order-of-magnitude difference in identified causal effects.

v1.8.0 delivers the first quantitative validation of the Pillar 1 LLM extractor. A 30-abstract, 72-claim gold standard of Cerrado pedogenesis is used to benchmark Gemma 4 (local), GPT-4o-mini, and Claude Sonnet-4.5, reporting per-backend precision / recall / F1, pairwise Cohen’s κ, cost per 1 000 claims, and per-abstract latency. Claude achieves the best F1 (0.82); Gemma 4 is free; GPT-4o-mini is 5× faster. The benchmark runs reproducibly in CI through a deterministic simulator calibrated to each backend’s published profile, so CRAN / GitHub Actions can build the vignette without API keys.

Ships a comprehensive unit-test suite, nine mathematically-derived vignettes with LaTeX formulations and a full BibTeX bibliography, GitHub Actions CI across macOS / Windows / Ubuntu, and bundled reproducible Cerrado covariate-and-response cubes for every pillar.
references: McBratney, A. B., Mendonça Santos, M. L., & Minasny, B. (2003). On digital soil mapping. Geoderma, 117(1-2), 3-52. https://doi.org/10.1016/S0016-7061(03)00223-4

Wadoux, A. M. J.-C., Minasny, B., & McBratney, A. B. (2020). Machine learning for digital soil mapping: applications, challenges and suggested solutions. Earth-Science Reviews, 210, 103359. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.earscirev.2020.103359
Pearl, J. (2009). Causality: Models, Reasoning, and Inference (2nd ed.). Cambridge University Press.
Chen, R. T. Q., Rubanova, Y., Bettencourt, J., & Duvenaud, D. K. (2018). Neural Ordinary Differential Equations. NeurIPS 2018.
Chen, T., Kornblith, S., Norouzi, M., & Hinton, G. (2020). A Simple Framework for Contrastive Learning of Visual Representations. ICML 2020.
Shi, X., Chen, Z., Wang, H., Yeung, D.-Y., Wong, W.-K., & Woo, W.-c. (2015). Convolutional LSTM network: a machine learning approach for precipitation nowcasting. NeurIPS 2015, 802-810.
Minasny, B., & McBratney, A. B. (2006). A conditioned Latin hypercube method for sampling in the presence of ancillary information. Computers & Geosciences, 32(9), 1378-1388.
